intro_band.agent_remit_summary: uAgents is Fetch.ai's Python framework for building autonomous agents that each hold a cryptographic identity and a blockchain wallet, register their address and endpoints on the Almanac smart contract, and exchange typed messages with other agents over authenticated channels. The framework's own security remit is to hand every agent built on it a trustworthy foundation: protected key material, cryptographically verified and replay-resistant inter-agent messaging, schema-validated inputs, and administrative surfaces reachable only by the local operator. Private keys must never be written to disk in plaintext, unverified senders must never be trusted for security decisions, and no remote party may reach the agent's introspection or control endpoints. This analysis judges the framework runtime's default behavior, not any single deployed agent.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A pure-Python framework split across the runtime package (`uagents/`) and a shared core (`uagents-core/`), using pydantic models, an ECDSA/secp256k1 identity (`uagents_core/identity.py`), and a bech32 address scheme. Inbound messages arrive over a uvicorn ASGI server in `asgi.py` that binds `HOST = "0.0.0.0"`, verifies envelope signatures for agent senders, and dispatches schema-validated payloads through `dispatch.py` to decorator-registered handlers in `agent.py`. Key material for name-based agents is persisted in plaintext to `private_keys.json` in the working directory via `storage/__init__.py`; agent state lives in a plaintext `KeyValueStore`. The `Agent` constructor enables the agent inspector by default (`enable_agent_inspector=True`), exposing reserved REST endpoints (`/messages`, `/connect`, `/disconnect`, `/agent_info`) whose loopback restriction relies on `scope["client"]` while the server is configured with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is safe cryptographic primitives whose guarantees are undercut by defaults and missing enforcement. Envelope signature verification and pydantic schema validation are real and on by default — `on_message` handlers reject unverified agent senders (`allow_unverified=False`) — but the "cryptographically secured" promise leaks at three seams: inbound signed envelopes are never checked against their own `expires`/`nonce` fields, so any captured envelope can be replayed; the loopback-only gate on the admin/inspector endpoints is defeated by `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, which lets a caller assert `X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1`; and name-based agents write their wallet and identity private keys to `private_keys.json` in plaintext in the working directory. The single most consequential item is that plaintext key storage, since those keys control on-chain assets and directly contradict the framework's headline security claim.

rules.rule_text: Receive inbound messages over an HTTP endpoint (and/or Agentverse mailbox/proxy), authenticate them, validate their payloads against declared message schemas, and dispatch them to the matching typed handler.
Create and manage a per-agent cryptographic identity (signing keypair) and a Fetch.ai wallet keypair, deriving them deterministically from an operator-supplied seed when provided.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.
Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Register the agent's address, endpoints, and protocol manifest on the Almanac contract and keep the registration current.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.
An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

findings.summary: Name-based agents persist identity and wallet private keys to plaintext private_keys.json in the working directory by default
findings.description: When an agent is created with a name and no seed, get_or_create_private_keys writes the identity private key and the Fetch.ai wallet private key as plaintext JSON to private_keys.json in the current working directory, with default file permissions and no encryption. These keys control the agent's on-chain identity and wallet assets, so plaintext at-rest storage directly contradicts the framework's "wallets are cryptographically secured" claim and exposes funds to any local user, backup, or accidental commit.
findings.policy_rule_text: Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: save_private_keys() writes {"identity_key": ..., "wallet_key": ...} via json.dump to private_keys.json with no encryption; path is os.getcwd()/private_keys.json (line 125)
"Otherwise the agent's private key will be stored locally alongside its name in private_keys.json" — documents plaintext key persistence as the default no-seed behavior

recommended_actions: Store identity and wallet keys through an encrypted-at-rest mechanism (OS keychain, an encrypted keystore, or a secret manager) instead of a plaintext file, and at minimum create private_keys.json with 0600 permissions in storage/__init__.py save_private_keys().
Make the seed-based path (Identity.from_seed, no key file) the documented default in the README quickstart so operators are steered away from plaintext key persistence.

findings.summary: forwarded_allow_ips="*" makes the loopback-only gate on reserved admin/inspector endpoints spoofable by any remote caller
findings.description: The ASGI server is configured with forwarded_allow_ips="*", so uvicorn rewrites scope["client"] from an attacker-controlled X-Forwarded-For header. The reserved-endpoint guard then allows /connect, /disconnect, and /messages only when "127.0.0.1" is in scope["client"], a check a remote caller defeats by sending X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1. This lets an unauthenticated remote party reach connect/disconnect control and the message-history endpoint that were intended for the local operator only.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn.Config(..., forwarded_allow_ips="*") — trusts X-Forwarded-For from any client, letting scope["client"] be spoofed
reserved-endpoint guard allows only when '"127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]' is false — a spoofed forwarded IP bypasses it; /agent_info is additionally exempted at line 305

recommended_actions: Set forwarded_allow_ips to the specific trusted proxy addresses (or leave it unset) so scope["client"] reflects the real peer, and gate reserved endpoints on the actual transport peer rather than a header-derived value.
Require an authentication token for the inspector/reserved endpoints instead of relying solely on source-IP equality.

findings.summary: Inbound signed envelopes are never checked against their expires/nonce fields, so a captured envelope can be replayed
findings.description: The envelope digest binds expires and nonce into the signature, but the receiving server verifies only the signature and never compares env.expires to the current time or tracks env.nonce/session for inbound messages, so a previously delivered signed envelope replays successfully and re-invokes the handler. The expires field is consulted only to size the sync-response wait timeout, not to reject stale envelopes, and there is no nonce/session dedup store anywhere in the dispatch path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.

evidence.snippet: after env.verify() the message is dispatched at line 379 with no check that env.expires > now and no nonce/session replay tracking
_digest() folds expires and nonce into the signed hash, but verify() only checks the signature — nothing enforces freshness or single-use on receipt

recommended_actions: In asgi.py before dispatch, reject envelopes whose expires timestamp is in the past, and maintain a bounded seen-nonce/session cache to drop replays.
Make expires mandatory (non-null) for signed inbound envelopes so every accepted message carries an enforced freshness window.

findings.summary: Agent HTTP server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default and returns wildcard CORS for all origins
findings.description: HOST is hardcoded to "0.0.0.0" so every agent's message server listens on all network interfaces by default with no operator opt-in or loopback default, and the server responds with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * plus wildcard allowed methods and headers. This maximizes default network exposure of the agent and its endpoints; while message handlers still require signature verification, it broadens the reachable attack surface (including the spoofable reserved-endpoint gate) beyond what a default deployment likely intends.
findings.policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

evidence.snippet: HOST = "0.0.0.0" — hardcoded bind address used in uvicorn.Config(host=HOST, ...) with no parameter to restrict to loopback
CORS headers set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*", Access-Control-Allow-Methods "GET, POST, OPTIONS", Access-Control-Allow-Headers "*"

recommended_actions: Default the bind host to 127.0.0.1 (or expose a host parameter on Agent) and require explicit operator opt-in to bind a public interface.
Scope CORS to the origins an agent actually needs rather than a blanket wildcard.

findings.summary: Agent inspector is enabled by default and /agent_info is served to any caller without the loopback gate
findings.description: The Agent constructor sets enable_agent_inspector=True by default, registering the reserved REST endpoints and keeping an in-memory envelope cache. The /agent_info endpoint is explicitly excluded from the loopback restriction, so any unauthenticated remote caller can retrieve the agent's metadata, endpoints, and protocol inventory, and the remaining reserved endpoints are protected only by the spoofable source-IP gate covered in PRAX-2026-07-10-002.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.

evidence.snippet: enable_agent_inspector: bool = True — default enables /messages, /connect, /disconnect and /agent_info REST handlers (registered at agent.py lines 472-540)
'request_path in set(RESERVED_ENDPOINTS) - {"/agent_info"}' — /agent_info is exempted from the localhost-only guard, disclosing agent metadata to any caller

recommended_actions: Default enable_agent_inspector to False, requiring explicit operator opt-in before any reserved/introspection endpoint is registered.
Apply the same authenticated, non-spoofable local-only gate to /agent_info as the other reserved endpoints, or reduce the metadata it returns to unauthenticated callers.

findings.summary: User-address senders skip signature verification, so ctx.sender is attacker-controlled for any unsigned handler
findings.description: The server skips envelope verification whenever the sender is a user address, and user addresses are self-generated random bech32 strings with no key binding. For handlers registered with allow_unverified=True (or any query-style user interaction) the sender field is unauthenticated and freely spoofable, so any handler that makes a trust or authorization decision on ctx.sender for user senders can be deceived. The framework's allow_unverified=False default mitigates this for standard signed handlers, but the unauthenticated sender value is surfaced identically to a verified one.
findings.policy_rule_text: Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.

evidence.snippet: 'if not is_user_address(env.sender): env.verify()' — signature verification is skipped entirely for user-address senders before dispatch
generate_user_address() returns _encode_bech32("user", token_bytes(32)) — a user address is unbound random bytes, so the sender identity carries no cryptographic proof

recommended_actions: Document prominently that ctx.sender is unauthenticated for user-address senders and must not be used for authorization, and surface a verified/unverified flag on the message context.
Provide a first-class authenticated-user mechanism (e.g., signed user envelopes) so handlers can require proof of user identity when needed.

findings.summary: get_or_create_private_keys persists a different wallet key than it returns, so a name-based agent's wallet is non-deterministic across restarts
findings.description: In the name-based key path, wallet_key is generated once and returned to the caller, but save_private_keys is called with a second, freshly generated PrivateKey().private_key rather than wallet_key, so the wallet key held in memory is never the one written to disk. On the next run the agent loads the persisted (different) wallet key, changing its wallet address and potentially stranding any funds sent to the first-run address. This is a correctness defect in the framework's own key management that undermines the "manage a wallet keypair" duty.
findings.policy_rule_text: Create and manage a per-agent cryptographic identity (signing keypair) and a Fetch.ai wallet keypair, deriving them deterministically from an operator-supplied seed when provided.

evidence.snippet: wallet_key = PrivateKey().private_key then save_private_keys(name, identity_key, PrivateKey().private_key) at line 149 — persists a new random key, discarding wallet_key, before returning wallet_key

recommended_actions: Change the save call to persist the same wallet_key that is returned (save_private_keys(name, identity_key, wallet_key)) so the in-memory and on-disk wallet keys match.
Add a regression test that asserts a name-based agent's wallet address is stable across two constructions.

findings.summary: No default inbound rate limiting on the message endpoint, leaving agents open to resource exhaustion
findings.description: The ASGI /submit path validates, verifies, and dispatches every inbound envelope with no request throttling, per-sender quota, or concurrency cap in the default configuration; rate limiting exists only in an opt-in experimental QuotaProtocol that a developer must wire in explicitly. A remote party can therefore flood an agent with envelopes (signature-verifying each one is itself CPU work), driving unbounded consumption of compute and any downstream LLM/tool calls a handler triggers.

evidence.snippet: dispatcher.dispatch_msg is called for every accepted envelope with no rate/quota/concurrency check anywhere in the __call__ request path
rate limiting is provided only as an opt-in experimental QuotaProtocol, not applied to the default inbound path

recommended_actions: Add a configurable default inbound rate limit / per-sender quota at the ASGI layer, applied before signature verification work is done.
Promote the QuotaProtocol out of experimental and document enabling it as a baseline deployment step.

positives.title: Default signature verification of inbound agent messages
positives.description: Inbound envelopes from agent addresses are cryptographically verified before dispatch and rejected on failure, and message handlers default to rejecting unverified senders (`allow_unverified=False`), so an agent must opt in to accept unauthenticated messages.

positives.title: Deterministic seed-derived identity with no plaintext key write
positives.description: When the operator supplies a seed, identity and wallet keys are derived deterministically (`Identity.from_seed`) and no key file is written, giving a safe key-handling path that avoids the plaintext `private_keys.json` persistence.

positives.title: Payload schema validation before handler dispatch
positives.description: Every inbound message is parsed and validated against its registered pydantic model before the handler runs, and a schema mismatch returns a structured error rather than reaching handler logic.

positives.title: Pinned dependency set with committed lockfile
positives.description: The package ships a committed `uv.lock` and uses upper-bounded version ranges for its runtime dependencies, limiting exposure to dependency-confusion and silent version-swap attacks.

rows.content_type: structured per-message JSON entries (sender, target, session, schema_digest, payload)
rows.purpose: optional message-history record, capped at 1000 entries / 86400s retention; written only when store_message_history is enabled
operational logging; a file sink is created only when the operator passes a log_file path, otherwise logs go to stdout

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial posture (floor 2) overall. The framework earns real credit in Implement Zero Trust for default ECDSA signature verification of inbound agent envelopes and pydantic schema validation before dispatch, and in Manage Your Supply Chain for a committed `uv.lock` and bounded dependency ranges — but Zero Trust is capped at Partial by plaintext key persistence, absent replay protection, and a spoofable loopback gate. Limit Your Domain and Balance Your Knowledge Base sit at Partial because typed-handler routing and schema validation constrain the message surface even though the framework imposes no scope restriction. Build an AI Red Team is Ad hoc — a broad functional test suite exists (including signature-verification tests) but there is no evidence of adversarial or injection testing, and Monitor Continuously is Partial on the strength of an optional structured `EnvelopeHistory` that is non-durable by default.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Dispatch is keyed by message-schema digest and messages with an unrecognized digest are dropped (`agent.py` `_process_single_message`), which structurally narrows what an agent will process; however, the framework imposes no topic or scope restriction and agents are general-purpose by construction.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The framework carries no LLM/RAG layer of its own, but it does validate every inbound payload against a declared pydantic model before the handler runs (`agent.py` `model_class.parse_raw`), giving a genuine input-schema boundary; it performs no semantic content sanitization, which is left to the agent developer.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: ECDSA envelope signature verification runs by default for agent senders and unverified agent messages are rejected (`asgi.py` `env.verify()`, `on_message(allow_unverified=False)`), but the control set is holed by plaintext key persistence, no inbound `expires`/`nonce` replay enforcement, a `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` spoofable loopback gate, a default `0.0.0.0` bind with wildcard CORS, and no inbound rate limiting.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed `uv.lock` and upper-bounded dependency ranges in `pyproject.toml` plus a documented version-compatibility matrix give established supply-chain hygiene; minor gaps are one floor-only pin (`cosmpy >=0.12.1`) and no published SBOM/ML-BOM.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A substantial functional test suite exists under `tests/` (including `test_msg_verify.py`, `test_server.py`, `test_registration.py`), but there is no adversarial, fuzzing, or injection/replay test evidence and nothing showing security testing drove design changes.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: An opt-in `EnvelopeHistory` records structured, per-message entries (sender, target, session, schema, payload) with a size/retention cap, but it is in-memory and non-durable by default, and the baseline `get_logger` emits only free-form INFO to stdout with no action-level or alerting layer.
